did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a hybrid DeepLabV3+ model with a ResNet-18 backbone architecture (Deep Convolutional Neural Network) for semantic segmentation of skin lesions and multiclass detection (skin, melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, and nevus) on the ISIC 2017 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving a proposed pipeline for semantic segmentation of skin lesions using a hybrid DeepLabV3+ model with ResNet-18 backbone. It details specific methodology (artifact removal, harmonic inpainting, class weighting), describes experiments conducted on the ISIC 2017 dataset, and reports new quantitative results (accuracy of 0.9143, wIoU of 0.86307). It is published in the proceedings of the 7th International Conference on Video, Signal and Image Processing (VSIP 2025).

title: Semantic Segmentation of Skin Lesions Using Deep CNNs with Artifact Removal and Multiclass Detection
journal: The 7th International Conference on Video, Signal and Image Processing (VSIP 2025)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin lesion segmentation by proposing a pipeline that integrates artifact removal with multiclass semantic segmentation using a hybrid DeepLabV3+ model with a ResNet-18 backbone. The method employs morphological operators for artifact detection and harmonic inpainting for restoration, followed by segmentation into four classes: skin, melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, and nevus. Experiments were conducted on the ISIC 2017 Challenge Dataset, which includes 2000 training images, 600 validation pairs, and 150 testing pairs. To address class imbalance, the authors implemented class weighting during the training process. The proposed model achieved a global accuracy of 0.9143 and a weighted intersection over union (wIoU) score of 0.86307. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach in handling visual artifacts and class imbalance, outperforming baseline models and previous ISIC leaderboard benchmarks.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the four classes used in the multiclass semantic segmentation task on the ISIC 2017 dataset. It is a malignant skin cancer class, noted as a minority class with 5% pixel count in the dataset.
One of the four classes used in the multiclass semantic segmentation task on the ISIC 2017 dataset. It is a benign lesion class, noted as a minority class with 2% pixel count in the dataset.
One of the four classes used in the multiclass semantic segmentation task on the ISIC 2017 dataset. It is a benign lesion class with 12% pixel count in the dataset.

One of the four classes used in the multiclass semantic segmentation task on the ISIC 2017 dataset, representing the background or healthy skin tissue. It is the majority class with 81% pixel count in the dataset.
An additional class used in the multiclass segmentation to capture transitional regions or ambiguous pixels, reflecting variability and uncertainty in dermatological images.

all_task_types.target: Semantic segmentation mask assigning each pixel to one of five classes: skin, melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, nevus, or undefined class.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'semantic segmentation of skin lesions' using a hybrid DeepLabV3+ model with ResNet-18 backbone. The task involves per-pixel classification into multiple discrete categories (multiclass detection) to delineate lesion boundaries and subtypes, rather than predicting a single diagnostic label (classification) or a continuous value (regression). Evaluation metrics used are global accuracy and weighted Intersection over Union (wIoU), which are standard for segmentation tasks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available database of dermoscopic images used for skin lesion analysis. The dataset includes images in JPEG format and corresponding superpixel masks in PNG format with EXIF data stripped. The ground truth masks were identified by two dermatologists simultaneously and are considered the gold standard.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid DeepLabV3+ model with ResNet-18 backbone, including artifact removal and semantic segmentation.
use.validation: Used for validation of the model performance.
use.testing: Used for testing the model performance.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To address class imbalance in the dataset during the training process, ensuring balanced learning for minority classes (e.g., melanoma, seborrheic keratosis).
To evaluate segmentation performance by accounting for class imbalance, providing a fairer assessment than standard IoU for datasets with unequal class distributions.
To quantify the overall proportion of correctly classified pixels across the entire dataset.
To assess how accurately the predicted object boundaries match the ground-truth boundaries, focusing on edge precision and recall.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, wIoU, BF Score) and compared against baseline models and ISIC leaderboard benchmarks without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2017 Challenge Dataset.notes: The proposed Hybrid DeepLabV3+ with ResNet-18 model outperformed baseline models and ISIC leaderboard benchmarks. It achieved a global accuracy of 0.9143 and a weighted Intersection over Union (wIoU) of 0.86307. The model demonstrated competitive performance in handling class imbalance and visual artifacts, particularly in delineating lesion margins for melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, and nevus.

architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet-18 backbone used with pre-trained weights

architecture_details.context_aggregation: Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (ASPP) with parallel dilated convolutions
architecture_details.feature_fusion: Fusion of high-level semantic features from ASPP with low-level features from early convolutional layers

training_details.loss_function: Dynamically Weighted Balanced Loss (DWBL) / Dynamic weighting scheme for class imbalance
training_details.hardware: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3080 Ti GPU with 32GB RAM

classification_or_output_head.class_weights: Dynamic weighting scheme applied to loss function to handle class imbalance

preprocessing_techniques: Color space transformation (RGB to indexed image)
Artifact removal using morphological operations (median and bottom-hat filtering in RGB channels, morphological opening, small object removal)
Harmonic inpainting for global smooth restoration of occluded areas (e.g., hair removal)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training as part of image augmentation parameters.
Applied during training as part of image augmentation parameters.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid DeepLabV3+ model with a ResNet-18 backbone, integrated with artifact removal and class weighting, achieved a global accuracy of 0.9143 and a weighted Intersection over Union (wIoU) of 0.86307 on the ISIC 2017 dataset.

The model outperformed baseline architectures including Hybrid U-net with ResNet-18 (wIoU 0.82555), SegNet (wIoU 0.7871), and previous ISIC leaderboard benchmarks such as CDNN (wIoU 0.784) and MaskRcnn2+segmentation (wIoU 0.802).

Integrating harmonic inpainting and morphological operators for artifact removal significantly improved boundary detection and lesion clarity by restoring areas affected by occlusions like hair and uneven illumination.

The use of a dynamic class weighting scheme effectively mitigated bias toward majority classes (skin), ensuring adequate learning of minority classes such as melanoma and seborrheic keratosis, which is critical for handling class imbalance in medical imaging.

Multiclass semantic segmentation provides clinical utility by enabling the categorization of lesion subtypes (melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, nevus, skin, and undefined class), supporting dermatologists in preliminary assessments and decision-making, particularly in differentiating difficult cases like seborrheic keratosis from melanoma.
The undefined class proved valuable in capturing transitional regions or ambiguous pixels, reflecting real-world variability and uncertainty in dermatological images.

The framework demonstrates potential as a low-cost, scalable, AI-driven decision-support system for teledermatology and resource-limited regions, though real-world deployment requires integration with diverse skin tones, imaging devices, and lesion types, particularly in underrepresented populations.

Interpretability remains a barrier to clinical adoption, with the authors noting that regulatory and ethical considerations demand more transparent systems beyond current visualization techniques like saliency maps.

Future work should focus on real-time deployment on mobile or cloud platforms, expanding training to larger multicenter datasets to enhance generalizability, and combining semantic segmentation with longitudinal patient data for dynamic disease progression tracking.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2017 dataset for training, validation, and testing, with no external validation on independent or diverse clinical datasets, limiting generalizability.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, with the 'Skin' class comprising 81% of pixels while 'Melanoma' and 'Seborrheic Keratosis' account for only 5% and 2% respectively; the paper addresses this via dynamic class weighting but does not report performance metrics per class.
The paper does not report statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the comparison against baseline models or ISIC leaderboard benchmarks.

The model was trained on images with EXIF data stripped and superpixel masks identified by two dermatologists; however, the paper acknowledges that real-world deployment requires validation across diverse skin tones and imaging devices, particularly for underrepresented populations like Asians and darker-skinned individuals.

Interpretability is identified as a barrier to clinical adoption, with the authors noting that current visualization techniques (saliency maps) are insufficient for regulatory and ethical requirements, and future work must focus on transparent systems.
The study is published in a conference proceeding (VSIP 2025) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny compared to journal publications.

The 'Undefined Class' was introduced to capture transitional regions, reflecting variability in dermatological images, but its clinical utility and impact on diagnostic accuracy for specific lesions are not quantified.
